questions.question_text: Find the angle between the hour hand and the minute hand of a clock when the time is 3.25.
questions.solution:
Angle traced by the hour hand in 12 hours = $360^{\circ}$.
Angle traced by it in 3 hrs 25 min., i.e.
$\frac{41}{12}$ hrs = $\left(\frac{360}{12}\times\frac{41}{12}\right)^{\circ} = 102\frac{1}{2}^{\circ}$.
Angle traced by minute hand in 60 min. = $360^{\circ}$.
Angle traced by it in 25 min. = $\left(\frac{360}{60}\times25\right)^{\circ} = 150^{\circ}$.
$\therefore$ Required angle = $\left(150^{\circ} - 102\frac{1}{2}^{\circ}\right) = 47\frac{1}{2}^{\circ}$.

questions.question_text: At what time between 2 and 3 o’clock will the hands of a clock be together?
questions.solution:
At 2 o’clock, the hour hand is at 2 and the minute hand is at 12, i.e. they are 10 min. spaces apart.
To be together, the minute hand must gain 10 minutes over the hour hand.
Now, 55 minutes are gained by it in 60 min.
$\therefore$ 10 minutes will be gained in $\left(\frac{60}{55}\times10\right)$ min. = $10\frac{10}{11}$ min.
$\therefore$ The hands will coincide at $10\frac{10}{11}$ min. past 2.

questions.question_text: At what time between 4 and 5 o’clock will the hands of a clock be at right angle?
questions.solution:
At 4 o’clock, the minute hand will be 20 min. spaces behind the hour hand.
Now, when the two hands are at right angles, they are 15 min. spaces apart.
So, they are at right angles in following two cases.
Case I. When minute hand is 15 min. spaces behind the hour hand :
In this case min. hand will have to gain (20 – 15) = 5 minute spaces.
55 min. spaces are gained by it in 60 min.
5 min. spaces will be gained by it in $\left(\frac{60}{55}\times5\right)$ min. = $5\frac{5}{11}$ min.
$\therefore$ They are at right angles at $5\frac{5}{11}$ min. past 4.
Case II. When the minute hand is 15 min. spaces ahead of the hour hand :
To be in this position, the minute hand will have to gain (20 + 15) = 35 minute spaces.
55 min. spaces are gained in 60 min.
35 min. spaces are gained in $\left(\frac{60}{55}\times35\right)$ min. = $38\frac{2}{11}$ min.
$\therefore$ They are at right angles at $38\frac{2}{11}$ min. past 4.

questions.question_text: Find at what time between 8 and 9 o’clock will the hands of a clock be in the same straight line but not together.
questions.solution:
At 8 o’clock, the hour hand is at 8 and the minute hand is at 12, i.e. the two hands are 20 min. spaces apart.
To be in the same straight line but not together they will be 30 minute spaces apart.
So, the minute hand will have to gain (30 – 20) = 10 minute spaces over the hour hand.
55 minute spaces are gained in 60 min.
10 minute spaces will be gained in $\left(\frac{60}{55}\times10\right)$ min. = $10\frac{10}{11}$ min.
$\therefore$ The hands will be in the same straight line but not together at $10\frac{10}{11}$ min. past 8.

questions.question_text: At what time between 5 and 6 o’clock are the hands of a clock 3 minutes apart?
questions.solution:
At 5 o’clock, the minute hand is 25 min. spaces behind the hour hand.
Case I. Minute hand is 3 min. spaces behind the hour hand.
In this case, the minute hand has to gain (25 – 3) = 22 minute spaces.
55 min. are gained in 60 min.
22 min. are gained in $\left(\frac{60}{55}\times22\right)$ min. = 24 min.
$\therefore$ The hands will be 3 min. apart at 24 min. past 5.
Case II. Minute hand is 3 min. spaces ahead of the hour hand.
In this case, the minute hand has to gain (25 + 3) = 28 minute spaces.
55 min. are gained in 60 min.
28 min. are gained in $\left(\frac{60}{55}\times28\right)$ min. = $31\frac{5}{11}$ min.
$\therefore$ The hands will be 3 min. apart at $31\frac{5}{11}$ min. past 5.

questions.question_text: The minute hand of a clock overtakes the hour hand at intervals of 65 minutes of the correct time. How much a day does the clock gain or lose?
questions.solution:
In a correct clock, the minute hand gains 55 min. spaces over the hour hand in 60 minutes.
To be together again, the minute hand must gain 60 minutes over the hour hand.
55 min. are gained in 60 min.
60 min. are gained in $\left(\frac{60}{55}\times60\right)$ min. = $65\frac{5}{11}$ min.
But, they are together after 65 min.
$\therefore$ Gain in 65 min. = $\left(65\frac{5}{11} - 65\right)$ min. = $\frac{5}{11}$ min.
Gain in 24 hours = $\left(\frac{5}{11}\times\frac{60\times24}{65}\right)$ min. = $10\frac{10}{43}$ min.
$\therefore$ The clock gains $10\frac{10}{43}$ minutes in 24 hours.

questions.question_text: A watch which gains uniformly, is 5 min. slow at 8 o’clock in the morning on Sunday and it is 5 min. 48 sec. fast at 8 p.m. on following Sunday. When was it correct?
questions.solution:
Time from 8 a.m. on Sunday to 8 p.m. on following Sunday = 7 days 12 hours = 180 hours.
$\therefore$ The watch gains $\left(5 + 5\frac{4}{5}\right)$ min. or $\frac{54}{5}$ min. in 180 hrs.
Now $\frac{54}{5}$ min. are gained in 180 hrs.
$\therefore$ 5 min. are gained in $\left(180\times\frac{5}{54}\times5\right)$ hrs. = 83 hrs 20 min. = 3 days 11 hrs 20 min.
$\therefore$ Watch is correct 3 days 11 hrs 20 min. after 8 a.m. of Sunday.
$\therefore$ It will be correct at 20 min. past 7 p.m. on Wednesday.

questions.question_text: A clock is set right at 5 a.m. The clock loses 16 minutes in 24 hours. What will be the true time when the clock indicates 10 p.m. on 4th day?
questions.solution:
Time from 5 a.m. on a day to 10 p.m. on 4th day = 89 hours.
Now 23 hrs 44 min. of this clock = 24 hours of correct clock.
$\therefore$ $\frac{356}{15}$ hrs of this clock = 24 hours of correct clock.
89 hrs of this clock = $\left(24\times\frac{15}{356}\times89\right)$ hrs of correct clock = 90 hrs of correct clock.
So, the correct time is 11 p.m.

questions.question_text: A clock is set right at 8 a.m. The clock gains 10 minutes in 24 hours. What will be the true time when the clock indicates 1 p.m. on the following day?
questions.solution:
Time from 8 a.m. on a day to 1 p.m. on the following day = 29 hours.
24 hours 10 min. of this clock = 24 hours of the correct clock.
$\frac{145}{6}$ hrs of this clock = 24 hrs of the correct clock.
29 hrs of this clock = $\left(24\times\frac{6}{145}\times29\right)$ hrs of the correct clock
= 28 hrs 48 min. of correct clock.
$\therefore$ The correct time is 28 hrs 48 min. after 8 a.m. This is 48 min. past 12.

questions.question_text: Rohan leaves home between 7 a.m. and 8 a.m. and returns between 1 p.m. and 2 p.m. to find that the minute and hour hands have interchanged their positions. How long was Rohan out of the house?
questions.solution:
Clearly, the hands will interchange positions after crossing each other 6 times i.e. they together will make (6 + 1) or 7 complete revolutions.
Since the hands interchange positions after every $55\frac{5}{13}$ minutes, we have:
Required time interval = $\left(55\frac{5}{13}\times7\right)$ min = $\left(\frac{720}{13}\times7\right)$ min = $\frac{5040}{13}$ min
= $387\frac{9}{13}$ min = 6 hrs $27\frac{9}{13}$ min.

questions.question_text: London time is five and a half hours behind Delhi time. What time is it in London if it is 0.2.35 in Delhi?
questions.solution: Clealy, time in London is 5 hrs 30 minutes behnd 2.35 a.m. which is 9.05 p.m. or 21.05 hrs.

questions.question_text: A bus leaves at 12.25 noon and reaches destination at 10.45 am. The duration of the journey is
questions.solution:
Duration of the journey
= (Duration form 12.25 noon to midnight)
+ (Duration from 12.00 midnight to 10.45 a.m.)
= 11 hrs 35 min + 10 hrs 45 min = 22 hrs 20 min.

questions.question_text: An accurate clock shows 8 o’ clock in the morning. Through how many degrees will the hour hand rotate when the clock shows 2 o’ clock in the afternoon?
questions.solution:
Angle traced by the hour hand in 6 hours
$=  rac{360}{12} 	imes 6 = 180^{\circ}$.

questions.question_text: How many rotations will the hour hand of a clock complete in 72 hours?
questions.solution: Number of rotations $=  rac{72}{12} = 6$.

questions.question_text: Through what angle does the minute hand of a clock turn in 5 minutes?
questions.solution:
Angle traced by the minute hand in 5 minutes
$=  rac{360}{60} 	imes 5 = 30^{\circ}$.

questions.question_text: In an accurate clock, in a period of 2 hours 20 minutes the minute hand will move over
questions.solution: Angle traced by the minute hand in 2 hrs 20 min, i.e., 140 min $=  rac{360}{60} 	imes 140 = 840^{\circ}$.

questions.question_text: A clock is started at noon. By 10 minutes past 5, the hour hand has turned through
questions.solution:
Angle traced by the hour hand in 12 hrs $= 360^{\circ}$.
Angle traced by the hour hand in 5 hrs 10 min, i.e., $ rac{31}{6}$ hrs $=  rac{360}{12} 	imes  rac{31}{6} = 155^{\circ}$.

questions.question_text: At 9:38 A.M, through how many degrees the hour hand of a clock moved since noon the previous day?
questions.solution:
Time from 12 noon to 9 : 38 A.M. $= 12$ hrs + 9 hrs 38 min $= 21$ hrs 38 min.
$= 21  rac{38}{60}$ hrs $= 21  rac{19}{30}$ hrs $=  rac{649}{30}$ hrs.
Angle traced by the hour hand in 12 hrs $= 360^{\circ}$.
Angle traced by the hour hand in $ rac{649}{30}$ hrs $=  rac{360}{12} 	imes  rac{649}{30} = 649^{\circ}$.

questions.question_text: At 3.40, the hour hand and the minute hand of a clock form an angle of
questions.solution:
Angle traced by hour hand in 12 hrs. $= 360^{\circ}$.
Angle traced by it in $10  rac{1}{3}$ hrs $=  rac{360}{12} 	imes  rac{31}{3} = 310^{\circ}$.
Angle traced by minute hand in 60 min. $= 360^{\circ}$.
Angle traced by it in 40 min. $=  rac{360}{60} 	imes 40 = 240^{\circ}$.
$\therefore$ Required angle $= (240 - 110)^{\circ} = 130^{\circ}$.

questions.question_text: The angle between the minute hand and the hour hand of a clock when the time is 8.30, is
questions.solution:
Angle traced by hour hand in $ rac{17}{2}$ hrs $=  rac{360}{12} 	imes  rac{17}{2} = 255^{\circ}$.
Angle traced by min. hand in 30 min. $=  rac{360}{60} 	imes 30 = 180^{\circ}$.
$\therefore$ Required angle $= (255 - 180)^{\circ} = 75^{\circ}$.

questions.question_text: The angle between the minute hand and the hour hand of a clock when the time is 4.20, is
questions.solution:
Angle traced by hour hand in $ rac{13}{3}$ hrs $=  rac{360}{12} 	imes  rac{13}{3} = 130^{\circ}$.
Angle traced by min. hand in 20 min. $=  rac{360}{60} 	imes 20 = 120^{\circ}$.
$\therefore$ Required angle $= (130 - 120)^{\circ} = 10^{\circ}$.

questions.question_text: At what angle the hands of a clock are inclined at 15 minutes past 5?
questions.solution:
Angle traced by hour hand in $ rac{21}{4}$ hrs $=  rac{360}{12} 	imes  rac{21}{4} = 157  rac{1}{2}^{\circ}$.
Angle traced by min. hand in 15 min. $=  rac{360}{60} 	imes 15 = 90^{\circ}$.
$\therefore$ Required angle $= 157  rac{1}{2}^{\circ} - 90^{\circ} = 67  rac{1}{2}^{\circ}$.

questions.question_text: The reflex angle between the hands of a clock at 10.25 is
questions.solution:
Angle traced by hour hand in $ rac{125}{12}$ hrs $=  rac{360}{12} 	imes  rac{125}{12} = 312  rac{1}{2}^{\circ}$.
Angle traced by minute hand in 25 min $=  rac{360}{60} 	imes 25 = 150^{\circ}$.
$\therefore$ Reflex angle $= 360^{\circ} - (312  rac{1}{2}^{\circ} - 150^{\circ}) = 360^{\circ} - 162  rac{1}{2}^{\circ} = 197  rac{1}{2}^{\circ}$.

questions.question_text: Match List I with List II and select the correct answer using the codes given below the lists:
questions.solution:
(A) Angle traced by hour hand in $ rac{7}{6}$ hrs $=  rac{360}{12} 	imes  rac{7}{6} = 35^{\circ}$.
Angle traced by minute hand in 10 min $=  rac{360}{60} 	imes 10 = 60^{\circ}$.
$\therefore$ Required angle $= (60^{\circ} - 35^{\circ}) = 25^{\circ}$.
(B) Angle traced by hour hand in $ rac{9}{4}$ hrs $=  rac{360}{12} 	imes  rac{9}{4} = 67  rac{1}{2}^{\circ}$.
Angle traced by minute hand in 15 min $=  rac{360}{60} 	imes 15 = 90^{\circ}$.
$\therefore$ Required angle $= 90^{\circ} - 67  rac{1}{2}^{\circ} = 22  rac{1}{2}^{\circ}$.
(C) Angle traced by hour hand in $ rac{26}{3}$ hrs $=  rac{360}{12} 	imes  rac{26}{3} = 260^{\circ}$.
Angle traced by minute hand in 40 min $=  rac{360}{60} 	imes 40 = 240^{\circ}$.
$\therefore$ Required angle $= (260^{\circ} - 240^{\circ}) = 20^{\circ}$.

questions.question_text: An accurate clock shows the time as 3.00. After the hour hand has moved $135^{\circ}$, the time would be
questions.solution:
Time taken by the hour hand to move $360^{\circ} = 12$ hours.
Time taken by the hour hand to move $135^{\circ} =  rac{12}{360} 	imes 135 = 4  rac{1}{2}$ hrs.
$\therefore$ Required time is $4  rac{1}{2}$ hrs ahead of 3.00, i.e., 7.30.

questions.question_text: What is the area of the face of a clock described by its minute hand between 9 a.m. and 9.35 a.m; if the minute hand is 10 cm long?
questions.solution:
Angle swept by the minute hand in 35 min $=  rac{360}{60} 	imes 35 = 210^{\circ}$.
$\therefore$ Required area = Area of a sector of a circle with radius 10 cm and central angle $210^{\circ} = \frac{\pi r^2 \theta}{360} = \frac{22}{7} \times 10 \times 10 \times \frac{210}{360} = \frac{550}{3} = 183  rac{1}{3} \text{ cm}^2$.

questions.question_text: The hands of a clock are 10 cm and 7 cm respectively. The difference between the distance traversed by their extremities in 3 days 5 hours is
questions.solution:
Number of rounds completed by the minute hand in 3 days 5 hrs $= (3 \times 24 + 5) = 77$.
Number of rounds completed by the hour hand in 3 days 5 hrs $= \frac{77}{12} = 6  rac{5}{12}$.
$\therefore$ Difference between the distance traversed $= \left[ 77 \times \left( 2 \times \frac{22}{7} \times 10 \right) - 6  rac{5}{12} \times \left( 2 \times \frac{22}{7} \times 10 \right) \right] \text{ cm} = (4840 - 282.33) \text{ cm} = 4557.67 \text{ cm}$.

questions.question_text: The minute arm of a clock is 10 cm long. The number of minutes taken by the tip of the arm to travel a length of 10 cm is nearly equal to
questions.solution:
Distance covered by the arm tip in 1 round $= 2 \times \frac{22}{7} \times 10 = \frac{440}{7} \text{ cm}$.
Now, $\frac{440}{7} \text{ cm}$ is covered in 60 min.
$\therefore 10 \text{ cm}$ is covered in $\frac{7}{440} \times 60 \times 10 \text{ min} = 9.54 \text{ min} \approx 10 \text{ min}$.

questions.question_text: At which time number of minutes elapsed since midnight is nine times the number of minutes before noon?
questions.solution:
Let the required time be $x$ minutes after midnight.
Duration from 12 midnight to 12 noon $= 12 \text{ hrs} = (12 \times 60) \text{ min} = 720 \text{ min}$.
So, $x = 9(720 - x) \Rightarrow 10x = 6480 \Rightarrow x = 648$.
Hence, required time $= 648 \text{ min}$ after midnight $= 10 \text{ hrs } 48 \text{ min}$ after midnight $= 10.48 \text{ a.m.}$

questions.question_text: How many times do the hands of a clock coincide in a day?
questions.solution:
The hands of a clock coincide 11 times in every 12 hours (Since between 11 and 1, the coincide only once, i.e. at 12 o’clock).
$\therefore$ The hands coincide 22 times in a day.

questions.question_text: How many times in a day, the hands of a clock are straight?
questions.solution:
In 12 hours, the hands coincide or are in opposite direction 
22 times.
∴ In 24 hours, the hands coincide or are in opposite 
direction 44 times a day.

questions.question_text: How many times are the hands of a clock at right angle in a day?
questions.solution:
In 12 hours, they are at right angles 22 times.
∴ In 24 hours, they are at right angles 44 times.

questions.question_text: How many times in a day, are the hands of a clock in straight line but opposite in direction?
questions.solution:
The hands of a clock point in opposite directions (in the 
same straight line) 11 times in every 12 hours (Because 
between 5 and 7 they point in opposite directions at 6 
o’clock only). So, in a day, the hands point in the opposite 
directions 22 times.

questions.question_text: How many times are the hour hand and the minute hand of a clock at right angles during their motion from 1.00 p.m. to 10.00 p.m.?
questions.solution:
The duration from 1.00 p.m. to 10.00 p.m. is 9 hours and 
during each of these 9 hours the hands of the clock are 
at right angles twice.
So, required number = $9 	imes 2 = 18$.

questions.question_text: A clock gains 5 minutes in one hour. Therefore, the angle traversed by the minute hand in 1 hour is
questions.solution:
Clearly, the minute hand traverses 65 minutes in 1 hour.
\ Required angle = $ rac{360}{60} 	imes 65 = 390^{\circ}$.

questions.question_text: A clock strikes once at 1O' clock, twice at 2O' clock, thrice at 3O' clock and soon. What is the total number of strikings in a day?
questions.solution:
Total number of strikings = $2 (1 + 2 + 3 + ........ + 12)$
$= 2 	imes  rac{12 	imes 13}{2} = 156$.

questions.question_text: A wall-clock takes 9 seconds in tringing at 9O' clock. The time, it will take in tringing at 11O' clock, is
questions.solution:
There are 8 intervals in 9 trings and 10 intervals in 11 
trings.
Time duration of 8 intervals = 9 sec.
\ Required time = Duration of 10 intervals
$=  rac{9}{8} 	imes 10$ sec $= 11.25$ sec.

questions.question_text: If a clock strikes six times in 5 seconds, the number of strikes in 10 seconds is
questions.solution:
There are 5 intervals in 6 strikes.
Number of intervals in 5 seconds = 5. Number of intervals 
in 10 seconds = 10.
So, the clock will strike 11 times in 10 seconds.

questions.question_text: In every 30 minutes the time of a watch increases by 3 minutes. After showing the correct time at 5 a.m; what time will the watch show after 6 hours?
questions.solution:
Time gained in 1 hour = 6 min.
Time gained in 6 hours = $(6 	imes 6)$ min = 36 min.
After 6 hours, the correct time is 11:00 a.m. and the watch 
will show 11:36 a.m.

questions.question_text: A watch becomes fast by 5 minutes everyday. By what percent does it become fast?
questions.solution:
Number of minutes in a day = $(24 	imes 60) = 1440$.
\ Required percentage = $ rac{5}{1440} 	imes 100 =  rac{50}{144} \%$.

questions.question_text: A clock goes slow from midnight by 5 minutes at the end of the first hour, by 10 minutes at the end of the second hour, by 15 minutes at the end of the third hour and so on. What will be the time by this clock after 6 hours?
questions.solution:
Time lost in 1 hour = 5 min.
Time lost in 6 hours = $(5 	imes 6)$ min = 30 min.
After 6 hours, the correct time will be 6 a.m. and the 
clock will show 5.30 a.m.

questions.question_text: A watch goes fast by 15 minutes compared to the right time everyday. If it is corrected and set to the standard time at 120' clock at noon, which of the following will be the time shown by it at 4 : 00 a.m. in the morning?
questions.solution:
Duration from 12 noon to 4:00 a.m. = 16 hours.
Time gained in 24 hours = 15 min.
Time gained in 16 hours = $ rac{5}{24} 	imes 16$ min = 10 min.
Hence, time shown by the watch at 4 a.m. = 4 : 10 a.m.

questions.question_text: It is between 3 P.M. and 4 P.M. and the distance between the hour and the minute hand of clock is 18 minute spaces. What time does the clock show?
questions.solution:
At 3 o’ clock, the minute hand is 15 min spaces behind 
the hour hand.
Thus, the minute hand has to gain $(15 + 18) = 33$ minute 
spaces.
55 min are gained in 60 min.
33 min are gained in $ rac{60}{55} 	imes 33 = 24$ min.
\ The hands will be 18 min spaces apart at 3 : 36 P.M.

questions.question_text: At what time between 9 and 10 o'clock are the hands of a clock 23 minute spaces apart?
questions.solution:
At 9 O' clock, the minute hand is 4 min. spaces behind 
the hour hand.
Thus, the minute hand has to gain $(45 - 23)$
$= 22$ min spaces.
5 min are gained in 60 min.
22 min are gained in $ rac{60}{55} 	imes 22 = 24$ min.
\ The hands will be 23 min spaces apart at 9 : 24.

questions.question_text: How much does a watch lose per day, if its hands coincide every 64 minutes?
questions.solution:
55 min. spaces are covered in 60 min.
60 min. spaces are covered in
$ rac{60}{55} 	imes 60$ min. $= 65  rac{5}{11}$ min.
Loss in 64 min. $= (65  rac{5}{11} - 64) =  rac{16}{11}$ min.
Loss in 24 hrs $=  rac{16}{11} 	imes  rac{1}{64} 	imes 24 	imes 60$ min. $= 32  rac{8}{11}$ min.

questions.question_text: At what time, in minutes, between 3 o'clock and 4 o'clock, both the needles will coincide each other?
questions.solution:
At 3 o’clock, the minute hand is 15 min. spaces apart 
from the hour hand.
To be coincident, it must gain 15 min. spaces.
55 min. are gained in 60 min.
15 min. are gained in $ rac{60}{55} 	imes 15 = 16  rac{4}{11}$ min.
\ The hands are coincident at $16  rac{4}{11}$ min. past 3.

questions.question_text: At what time between 9 and 10 o'clock will the hands of a watch be together?
questions.solution:
To be together between 9 and 10 o’clock, the minute hand 
has to gain 45 min. spaces.
55 min. spaces are gained in 60 min.
45 min. spaces are gained in $ rac{60}{55} 	imes 45 = 49  rac{1}{11}$ min.
\ The hands are together at $49  rac{1}{11}$ min. past 9.

questions.question_text: At what time between 7 and 8 o'clock will the hands of a clock be in the same straight line but, not together?
questions.solution:
When the hands of the clock are in the same straight line 
but not together, they are 30 minute spaces apart.
At 7 o’clock, they are 25 min. spaces apart.
\ Minute hand will have to gain only 5 min. spaces.
55 min. spaces are gained in 60 min.
5 min. spaces are gained in $ rac{60}{55} 	imes 5 = 5  rac{5}{11}$ min.
\ Required time = $5  rac{5}{11}$ min. past 7.

questions.question_text: At what time between 4 and 5 o'clock will the hands of a watch point in opposite directions?
questions.solution:
At 4 o’clock, the hands of the watch are 20 min. spaces 
apart.
To be in opposite directions, they must be 30 min. spaces 
apart.
\ Minute hand will have to gain 50 min. spaces.
55 min. spaces are gained in 60 min.
50 min. spaces are gained in $ rac{60}{55} 	imes 50 = 54  rac{6}{11}$ min.
\ Required time = $54  rac{6}{11}$ min. past 4.

questions.question_text: At what time between 5.30 and 6 will the hands of a clock be at right angles?
questions.solution:
At 5 o’clock, the hands are 25 min. spaces apart.
To be at right angles and that too between 5.30 and
6, the minute hand has to gain $(25 + 15) = 40$ min. spaces.
55 min. spaces are gained in 60 min.
40 min. spaces are gained in $ rac{60}{55} 	imes 40 = 43  rac{7}{11}$ min.
\ Required time = $43  rac{7}{11}$ min. past 5.

questions.question_text: Henry started a trip into the country between 8 A.M. and 9 A.M. when the hands of clock were together. He arrived at his destination between 2 P.M. and 3 P.M. when the hands of the clock were exactly $180^{\circ}$ apart. How long did he travel?
questions.solution:
To be together between 8 A.M. and 9 A.M, the minute hand has to gain 40 min spaces.
55 min. spaces are gained in 60 min.
40 min. spaces are gained in $\left(\frac{60}{55}\times40\right)$ min = $43\frac{7}{11}$ min.

questions.question_text: Imagine that your watch was correct at noon, but then it began to lose 30 minutes each hour. It now shows 4 p.m. but it stopped 5 hours ago. What is the correct time now?
questions.solution:
The watch loses $\frac{1}{2}$ hour each hour. So, it must have taken 8 hours to show 4 p.m. from 12 noon. Thus, it stopped at 8 p.m.
So, the correct time is 5 hours ahead of 8 p.m., i.e., 1 a.m.

questions.question_text: A mechanical grandfather clock is at present showing 7 hr 40 min 6 sec. Assuming that it loses 4 seconds in every hour, what time will it show after exactly $6\frac{1}{2}$ hours?
questions.solution:
Time lost in $6\frac{1}{2}$ hours = $\left(6\frac{1}{2}\times4\right)$ sec = 26 sec.
Correct time after $6\frac{1}{2}$ hours = 7 hr 40 min 6 sec + 6 hr 30 min = 14 hr 10 min 6 sec.
Time shown by the clock = 14 hr 10 min 6 sec – 26 sec = 14 hr 9 min 40 sec.

questions.question_text: I have two watches with a 12 hour cycle. One of them gains one minute a day and the other loses $1\frac{1}{2}$ minutes per day. If I set them both at the correct time, how long will it be before they again tell the correct time together?
questions.solution:
Clearly, the first watch will show the correct time when it has gained 12 hours i.e., $(12\times60) = 720$ min and the second watch will show the correct time when it has lost 720 min.
Time taken by first watch to gain 720 min = 720 days.
Time taken by second watch to gain 720 min = $\left(720\div\frac{3}{2}\right)$ days = 480 days.
So the first watch shows correct time after every 720 days and the second watch after every 480 days.
Time after which both the clocks will together tell the correct time = L.C.M. of 720 and 480 = 1440 days.

questions.question_text: A watch is 1 minute slow at 1 p.m. on Tuesday and 2 minutes fast at 1 p.m. on Thursday. When did it show the correct time?
questions.solution:
Time from 1 p.m. on Tuesday to 1 p.m. on Thursday = 48 hours.
So, the watch gains $(1 + 2)$ min or 3 min in 48 hrs.
Now, 3 min are gained in 48 hrs.
So, 1 min is gained in $\left(\frac{48}{3}\right) = 16$ hrs.
Thus, the watch showed the correct time 16 hrs. after 1 p.m. on Tuesday, i.e. 5 a.m. on Wednesday.

questions.question_text: A watch which gains uniformly is 2 minutes low at noon on Monday and is 4 min. 48 sec fast at 2 p.m. on the following Monday. When was it correct?
questions.solution:
Time from 12 p.m. on Monday to 2 p.m. on the following Monday = 7 days 2 hours = 170 hours.
$\therefore$ The watch gains $\left(2 + \frac{4}{5}\right)$ min. or $\frac{34}{5}$ min. in 170 hrs.
Now, $\frac{34}{5}$ min. are gained in 170 hrs.
$\therefore$ 2 min. are gained in $\left(\frac{5}{34}\times170\times2\right)$ hrs = 50 hrs.
$\therefore$ Watch is correct 2 days 2 hrs. after 12 p.m. on Monday i.e. it will be correct at 2 p.m. on Wednesday.

questions.question_text: A watch which gains 5 seconds in 3 minutes was set right at 7 a.m. In the afternoon of the same day, when the watch indicated quarter past 4 o'clock, the true time is
questions.solution:
Time from 7 a.m. to 4.15 p.m. = 9 hrs 15 min. = $\frac{37}{4}$ hrs.
3 min. 5 sec. of this clock = 3 min. of the correct clock.
$\Rightarrow \frac{37}{720}$ hrs of this clock = $\frac{1}{20}$ hrs of the correct clock
$\Rightarrow \frac{37}{4}$ hrs of this clock = $\left(\frac{1}{20}\times\frac{720}{37}\times\frac{37}{4}\right)$ hrs of the correct clock = 9 hrs of the correct clock
$\therefore$ The correct time is 9 hrs after 7 a.m., i.e. 4 p.m.

questions.question_text: Between 5 and 6, a lady looked at her watch and mistaking the hour hand for the minute hand, she thought that the time was 57 minutes earlier than the correct time. The correct time was
questions.solution:
Since the time read by the lady was 57 minutes earlier than the correct time, so the minute hand is $(60 - 57) = 3$ minute spaces behind the hour hand.
Now, at 5 o’clock, the minute hand is 25 minute spaces behind the hour hand.
To be 3 minute spaces behind, it must gain $(25 - 3) = 22$ minute spaces.
55 min spaces are gained in 60 min.
22 min spaces are gained in $\left(\frac{60}{55}\times22\right) = 24$ min.
Hence, the correct time was 24 minutes past 5.

questions.question_text: How much does a watch losse per day if its hands coincide every 64 minutes?
questions.solution:
55 minutes spaces are covered in 60 minutes
60 minutes spaces are covered in $\left[\frac{60}{55}\times60\right]$ minutes = $65\frac{5}{11}$ minutes
Loss in 64 minute = $\left(65\frac{5}{11} - 64\right) = \frac{16}{11}$ minutes
Loss in 24 hours = $\left(\frac{16}{11}\times\frac{1}{64}\times24\times60\right)$ minutes = $32\frac{8}{11}$ minutes

questions.question_text: Wall clock gains 2 minutes in 12 hours, while a table clock loses 2 minutes every 36 hours. Both are set right at 12 noon on Tuesday. The correct time when both show the same time next would be
questions.solution:
After 12 days, i.e., after $12\times24$ hours clock A will gain 48 minutes and will slow 12:48 noon.
After 12 days, i.e., after $12\times24$ hours clock B will loose 16 minutes and will show 11:44 am.
The two clocks will show the same time after 135 days
The time difference has to be 12 hours between then = 720 minutes.
A will gain 540 minutes in 135 days.
B will loose 180 minutes in 135 days Total 720 minutes.
Further if we consider only time then the problem becomes simpler
Total difference of minutes between the times shown by the clocks after 36 hours
$\Rightarrow \frac{16}{3}$ minutes difference in 1 day
$\Rightarrow 12\times60$ minutes difference in $\left(\frac{3}{16}\times12\times60\right) = 135$ days

questions.question_text: The angle between the hands of a clock when the time is 4 : 25 am is
questions.solution:
Let angle between the hands of clock be $x^\circ$.
When the time is 4 : 25 a.m.
{Where M = minutes and H = hours}
Required angle = $30\left(\frac{M}{5} - H\right) - \frac{M}{2}$
$= 30\left(\frac{25}{5} - 4\right) - \frac{25}{2} = 30(5 - 4) - 12.5 = 30 - 12.5 = 17.5^\circ = 17\frac{1}{2}^\circ$

questions.question_text: At 8:30, the hour hand and the minute hand of clock form an angle of
questions.solution:
In 1 hour, the hour hand’s make the angle of $30^\circ$.
The hour hand make the angle of $x^\circ$ in $8\frac{30}{60}$ hours.
$\Rightarrow x = \left(30\times8\frac{1}{2}\right)^\circ = \left(30\times\frac{17}{2}\right) = 225^\circ$
The minute hand make the angle in 1 minute = $6^\circ$
Minute hand makes the angle in 30 minutes = $(6\times30)^\circ = 180^\circ$
Required angle = $255^\circ - 180^\circ = 75^\circ$

questions.question_text: A clock is displaying correct time at 9am on Monday. If the clock loses 12 minutes in 24 hours, then the actual time when the clock indicates 8:30pm on Wednesday of the same week is
questions.solution:
Time interval from 9 am on Monday to 8 : 30 pm on Wednesday
= $(24\times2.5 - 0 : 30)$ hours
= 60 – 0 : 30 hours
= 59 hours 30 minutes
= $59\frac{30}{60} = 59\frac{1}{2} = \frac{119}{2}$ hours
Also 24 hours – 12 minutes
= 23 hours 48 minutes
= $23\frac{48}{60} = 23\frac{4}{5} = \frac{119}{5}$ hours
$\frac{119}{5}$ hours of this clock = 24 hours of the correct clock
$\therefore \frac{119}{2}$ hours of this clock = $\left(\frac{24\times5}{119}\times\frac{119}{2}\right) = 60$ hours
$\left(60 - \frac{119}{2}\right)$ hours = $\left(\frac{120 - 119}{2}\right)$ hours = $\frac{1}{2}$ hours = 30 minutes
Hence, the correct time is 30 minutes after 8 : 30 pm. i.e. 9 pm.

questions.question_text: There are two clocks, both set to show 10 pm on 21st January 2010. One clock gains 2 minutes in an hour and the other clock loses 5 minutes in an hour. Then by how many minutes do the two clocks differ at 4 pm on 22nd January 2010?
questions.solution:
One clock show 10pm. On 21st January 2010
One clock gains = 2 minutes
Other clock loses = 5 minutes
Time period between 10 pm and 4 pm = 18 hours
$\therefore$ Required difference = $(2\times18 + 5\times18)$ minutes
= 126 minutes
